# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define 1's complement and 2's complement of a binary number. Why is 2's complement preferred over 1's complement in modern digital systems for representing signed numbers?**

**Model Answer (Key Points Examiner Expects):**
- **1's complement** of an n-bit binary number N is obtained by inverting every bit; mathematically N₁ = (2ⁿ − 1) − N.
- **2's complement** is obtained by adding 1 to the 1's complement; mathematically N₂ = 2ⁿ − N.
- 2's complement is preferred because:
  1. It has only **one representation of zero** (cleaner logic, no +0/−0 ambiguity).
  2. Subtraction is simpler: just **add and discard carry-out** (no end-around carry needed as in 1's complement).
  3. It has a **wider negative range** (−2ⁿ⁻¹ vs −(2ⁿ⁻¹−1)).
  4. Hardware implementation is more straightforward (one adder suffices).

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is BCD? Why is BCD used even though it is less efficient than pure binary representation?**

**Model Answer:**
- BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) represents each decimal digit independently using a 4-bit binary code (0000 to 1001 for digits 0–9).
- It is used because:
  1. **Easy conversion to/from decimal** for human-readable displays (7-segment, LCD).
  2. **Avoids rounding errors** in financial/decimal-centric applications (banking, billing).
  3. **Simplifies decimal arithmetic circuits** like BCD adders and decimal counters.
  4. Trade-off: less storage-efficient — each decimal digit uses 4 bits, but only 10 of 16 states are used, wasting ~6/16 ≈ **37.5%** of the code space.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Perform the following arithmetic operations using 2's complement representation with 8-bit numbers. Show all steps clearly:
- (i) (+45) − (+18)
- (ii) (−25) − (+30)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the decimal number 478₁₀ to BCD. Then perform BCD addition of 286₁₀ + 547₁₀ and apply the +6 correction wherever necessary. Show all intermediate results.

---

### Model Solution for Question B-A

#### Part (a)(i): (+45) − (+18) in 8-bit 2's complement

**Step 1:** Write A = +45 in 8-bit binary.
- 45₁₀ = 32 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 2⁵ + 2³ + 2² + 2⁰
- A = **00101101₂**

**Step 2:** Write B = +18 in 8-bit binary.
- 18₁₀ = 16 + 2 = 2⁴ + 2¹
- B = **00010010₂**

**Step 3:** Compute 2's complement of B (= −18).
- 1's complement of B = 11101101
- 2's complement of B = 11101101 + 1 = **11101110₂**

**Step 4:** Add A and (−B)₂.
- 00101101
- + 11101110
- ─────────
- (1) 00011011   ← carry-out = 1 (discarded)

**Step 5:** Result = **00011011₂**.
- 00011011 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1 = **27₁₀** ✓
- Verification: 45 − 18 = 27. ✓

#### Part (a)(ii): (−25) − (+30) in 8-bit 2's complement

**Step 1:** A = −25 → take 2's complement of +25.
- +25₁₀ = 00011001₂
- 1's complement: 11100110
- 2's complement: 11100110 + 1 = **11100111₂** (= −25)

**Step 2:** B = +30 = **00011110₂**.

**Step 3:** Compute 2's complement of B (= −30).
- 1's complement of B = 11100001
- 2's complement of B = 11100001 + 1 = **11100010₂**

**Step 4:** Add A₂ + B₂.
- 11100111  (−25)
- + 11100010  (−30)
- ─────────
- (1) 11001001   ← carry-out = 1 (discarded)

**Step 5:** Result = **11001001₂**.
- MSB = 1 → result is negative.
- To find magnitude: 2's complement of result.
- 1's complement: 00110110
- Add 1: 00110111 = 32 + 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = **55₁₀**
- So result = **−55₁₀** ✓
- Verification: −25 − 30 = −55. ✓

#### Part (b): Convert 478₁₀ to BCD and perform 286 + 547 in BCD

**Step 1: Decimal → BCD conversion of 478.**
- Digit 4 = 0100
- Digit 7 = 0111
- Digit 8 = 1000
- **BCD (478) = 0100 0111 1000**

**Step 2: BCD representation of 286 and 547.**
- 286 = 0010 1000 0110
- 547 = 0101 0100 0111

**Step 3: BCD addition (units digit first).**

**Units:** 0110 + 0111 = 1101 = 13₁₀. Since 13 > 9, add 0110:
- 1101 + 0110 = 1 0011 → write 0011, carry 1 to tens.

**Tens:** 1000 + 0100 + 1(carry) = 1101 = 13₁₀. Since 13 > 9, add 0110:
- 1101 + 0110 = 1 0011 → write 0011, carry 1 to hundreds.

**Hundreds:** 0010 + 0101 + 1(carry) = 1000 = 8₁₀. 8 ≤ 9, so no correction:
- Write 1000, carry 0.

**Final BCD sum: 1000 0011 0011** → reads as **8, 3, 3** → **833₁₀**.

**Verification:** 286 + 547 = 833. ✓

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Missing the +6 correction** after BCD addition is the #1 mistake — if the raw sum is 1010 to 1111 (10–15) **or** a carry-out is produced, you **must** add 0110 to that nibble.
> - **Ignoring the carry** between decades (units → tens → hundreds) leads to wrong results.
> - **Interpreting the carry-out as a discarded bit** (as in 2's complement) is wrong for BCD — here the carry propagates to the next decade.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **double-dabble algorithm** for converting a BCD number to its binary equivalent. Apply the algorithm to convert BCD **0011 0100 1001** (= 349₁₀) to binary, showing all 10 shifts step-by-step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **1's complement** and **2's complement** representations of signed numbers. Perform the subtraction 25 − 47 using **8-bit 1's complement** method, clearly showing the end-around carry operation.

---

### Model Solution for Question B-B

#### Part (a): Double-Dabble Algorithm — Conceptual Explanation

**Definition:** The double-dabble algorithm (also called *shift-and-add-3*) converts a BCD number to binary by repeated left-shifts. Before each shift, any BCD nibble ≥ 5 is incremented by 3. This pre-correction ensures that when the shift pushes a 5–9 nibble into a "tens" position, the resulting binary accumulation is correct.

**Why the +3 before shift works:**
- A BCD nibble holding value 5, 6, 7, 8, or 9 will, when shifted left, push a '1' into the next-higher binary position, contributing at least 2⁴ = 16. But in BCD, that same value (5–9) should contribute only up to 9. Pre-adding 3 brings the nibble to 8, 9, 10, 11, 12 — so that the shift converts it to a binary-equivalent contribution.

**Flowchart (high-level):**
1. Load BCD number into BCD register (n nibbles).
2. Initialize binary register to 0 (k bits, where k = ⌈log₂(10ⁿ)⌉).
3. Repeat for (number of BCD bits) iterations:
   - For each BCD nibble: if value ≥ 5, add 3 to that nibble.
   - Shift the entire combined register (BCD + binary) left by 1.
4. After all shifts, the binary register contains the binary equivalent.

**Application to BCD 0011 0100 1001 (= 349₁₀):**

| Shift # | H (Hundreds) | T (Tens) | U (Units) | Bin(9 bits) | Pre-shift Correction |
|---------|--------------|----------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| Init    | 0011 (3)     | 0100 (4) | 1001 (9)  | 000000000   | —                    |
| 1       | 0110         | 1001     | 0010      | 000000000   | U was 9 ≥ 5 → +3 → 1100; shift happens next |
| 2       | 1001         | 1100     | 0100      | 000000000   | H=6≥5→+3→1001; T=9≥5→+3→1100; shift |
| 3       | 1100         | 1111     | 1000      | 000000000   | H=9≥5→+3→1100; T=12≥5→+3→1111; shift |
| 4       | 1111         | 1111     | 0000      | 000000000   | H=12≥5→+3→1111; T=15≥5→+3→...; shift |
| 5       | 1111         | 1110     | 0000      | 000000000   | T=11→keep; shift |
| ...     | ...          | ...      | ...       | ...         | ... |
| 10      | 0000         | 0000     | 0000      | 101011101   | Done |

**Final binary result: 101011101₂** = 256 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 1 = **349₁₀** ✓

> [!NOTE] **Verification**
> Reading the binary column at the end gives the answer. The BCD columns become 0000 as all data has shifted out into the binary register.

#### Part (b): 1's Complement vs 2's Complement + Subtraction Example

**Differences (tabular form for full marks):**

| Feature | 1's Complement | 2's Complement |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Zero representation | Two: +0 and −0 | One: 0 |
| Negative range | −(2ⁿ⁻¹−1) | −2ⁿ⁻¹ |
| Subtraction method | Add 1's comp; **add EAC to LSB** | Add 2's comp; **discard carry** |
| Hardware complexity | Slightly higher (extra adder for EAC) | Lower |
| Overflow detection | Less straightforward | XOR of MSB carries |
| Modern use | Legacy / academic | Industry standard |

**Subtraction: 25 − 47 using 8-bit 1's complement**

**Step 1:** A = +25 = 00011001₂.
**Step 2:** B = +47 = 00101111₂.
**Step 3:** 1's complement of B = 11010000₂ (this represents −47 in 1's complement).

**Step 4:** Add A and (B)₁:
- 00011001
- + 11010000
- ─────────
- 11101001   ← carry-out = 0 (no end-around carry needed this time)

**Step 5:** Result = **11101001₂** with MSB = 1 → negative.
- To find magnitude: 1's complement = 00010110 = 16 + 4 + 2 = 22₁₀.
- **Result = −22₁₀** ✓ (since 25 − 47 = −22).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b)**
> - **Forgetting the end-around carry** in 1's complement subtraction when carry-out = 1 → answer is off by 1.
> - **Misinterpreting 11101001** — students sometimes try to "take 2's complement" of a 1's complement negative number; that is wrong. Use **1's complement** to recover magnitude.
> - **Confusing sign-magnitude with 1's complement** — the bit pattern in 1's complement is NOT a sign-magnitude representation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams)**

- ☐ **1's complement**: bit-wise NOT; formula: N₁ = (2ⁿ − 1) − N.
- ☐ **2's complement**: (1's complement) + 1; formula: N₂ = 2ⁿ − N.
- ☐ **Method B for 2's comp**: scan from LSB, keep bits up to first 1, invert the rest.
- ☐ **2's complement range (n-bit)**: −2ⁿ⁻¹ to +(2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1).
- ☐ **1's complement has two zeros**; 2's complement has one zero.
- ☐ **Subtraction via 2's comp**: add + **discard** carry-out.
- ☐ **Subtraction via 1's comp**: add + **end-around carry** to LSB.
- ☐ **Sign bit = MSB**: 0 → positive, 1 → negative (in 2's comp).
- ☐ **Overflow detection (2's comp)**: V = Cₙ ⊕ Cₙ₋₁ (XOR of carry-in and carry-out of MSB position).
- ☐ **Sign extension**: replicate MSB when extending bit-width.
- ☐ **BCD = 4 bits per decimal digit**; only 0000–1001 valid.
- ☐ **Illegal BCD codes**: 1010, 1011, 1100, 1101, 1110, 1111.
- ☐ **BCD addition correction**: if nibble > 9 or carry-out, add 0110 (decimal 6).
- ☐ **Double-dabble / Shift-and-Add-3**: for BCD ↔ binary conversion.
- ☐ **Self-complementing codes**: 2-4-2-1 and Excess-3 (XS-3) — 9's complement by inversion.
- ☐ **Most-negative 2's comp number has no positive counterpart** (e.g., −128 in 8-bit); overflow on negation.
- ☐ **Arithmetic shift right (ASR)** = sign-preserving division by 2; **logical shift right (LSR)** = fills with 0.

> [!TIP] **Exam Strategy Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Always show **bit-by-bit addition with carries**. The examiner awards partial marks for every correctly shown bit and carry. A single final answer without intermediate work loses 40–60% of the marks even if the final value is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gray Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT402 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gray Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gray Code (Reflected Binary Code):** An ordering of the binary number system such that **only one bit changes state between any two successive code words**. It is a non-weighted, cyclic code widely used in digital systems to minimize errors during transitions.

A Gray code of $n$ bits generates $2^n$ unique code words, where every adjacent pair (and also the last to the first) differs in exactly **one bit position**. This single-bit-change property is the *defining* hallmark of the code.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Staircase Light Switch" Analogy)

Imagine walking up a staircase in a dark hallway. You have multiple light switches lined up on the wall:

* In a normal **Binary** counting system, if you have 4 switches and the count goes from `0111` (7) to `1000` (8), **all four switches flip at once**. If even one switch toggles a few microseconds *later* than the others, the system briefly reads a junk value like `0110`, `0100`, or `0000`. This is a **race condition** in digital logic.
* In **Gray code**, between `0111` and `1000` (which become `0100` and `1100` in Gray), **only one switch flips**. So the intermediate ambiguity is eliminated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications of Gray code include:**
> * **Karnaugh Maps (K-Maps):** Adjacent cells in a K-Map differ by exactly one bit — this is Gray code addressing.
> * **Shaft Encoders / Rotary Encoders:** Used in CNC machines, printers, and robotics to read angular position without ambiguity.
> * **Error Correction & Asynchronous FIFO pointers:** Prevents false reads during multi-bit transitions across clock domains.
> * **Analog-to-Digital Converters (Flash ADCs):** Reduces transient errors during sampling.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Code Type | **Non-weighted**, **Cyclic**, **Unit-distance** |
| Bit length $n$ | Produces $2^n$ unique code words |
| Hamming distance (adjacent) | **Exactly 1** |
| Self-complementary | **No** (in its basic form) |
| KTU weightage indicator | **High** — frequently asked in ESE & MSE |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Properties of Gray Code (Logic Bullet Stack)

* **Non-weighted:** No fixed positional weight (unlike BCD or binary). You cannot directly compute a decimal value by multiplying bits by $2^k$.
* **Cyclic:** The last code word and the first code word also differ in **exactly one bit**, forming a closed ring.
* **Unit-distance code:** Adjacent code words have a Hamming distance of 1.
* **Existence:** A Gray code sequence exists for *every* $n \geq 1$. It is constructed using the **reflect-and-prefix** (mirroring) algorithm.
* **Reflective Construction:** The $n$-bit Gray code is built from the $(n-1)$-bit Gray code by:
   1. Writing the $(n-1)$-bit sequence in original order, prefixing a **0**.
   2. Writing the $(n-1)$-bit sequence in **reverse** order, prefixing a **1**.
* **Uniqueness:** The reflected (canonical) Gray code is the *most commonly used*, but it is **not unique** — many other valid Gray codes exist for the same $n$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following two formulas are the *most exam-critical* and must be memorized verbatim for KTU 2024 ESE.

| Operation | Formula / Logic | Symbols Explained |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Binary $\rightarrow$ Gray** | $G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1}$ | $B_n$ is MSB; $G_n = B_n$ (MSB is preserved) |
| **Gray $\rightarrow$ Binary** | $B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1}$ | Recursive; $B_n = G_n$ (MSB preserved) |
| **Most Significant Bit** | $G_{n-1} = B_{n-1}$ | The MSB of Gray = MSB of Binary |
| **Cyclic Property** | $\text{Hamming}(G_0, G_{2^n-1}) = 1$ | First and last words also differ in 1 bit |
| **Count of code words** | $N = 2^n$ | For an $n$-bit Gray code |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the XOR symbol $\oplus$ represents the **Exclusive-OR** (modulo-2 addition). Do **not** use a vertical bar $\vert$ for absolute value inside a markdown table row — it breaks the cell. Always use $\oplus$ or write "XOR" in plain text.

## 2.3 Why This Code Matters in Real Engineering

* **K-Map Simplification:** Boolean functions are plotted on K-maps whose row/column labels follow Gray code order. This guarantees that *logically adjacent* minterms (differing by one variable) are also *physically adjacent* cells, enabling visual identification of prime implicants.
* **Rotary Encoders:** Optical or magnetic encoders on motor shafts use Gray-coded disks. As the shaft rotates, only **one track** transitions at a time, eliminating read errors.
* **Asynchronous FIFO Pointers:** Cross-clock-domain synchronization uses Gray-coded pointers to safely transfer read/write addresses, since only one bit toggles per increment/decrement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Binary-to-Gray Conversion Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** For an $n$-bit binary number $B = b_{n-1}\,b_{n-2}\ldots b_1\,b_0$, the corresponding Gray code $G = g_{n-1}\,g_{n-2}\ldots g_1\,g_0$ is given by:
>
> $$g_i = b_{i+1} \oplus b_i, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-2$$
>
> and the MSB rule:
>
> $$g_{n-1} = b_{n-1}$$

**Step-by-step proof (constructive, by mirror-prefix method):**

**Step 1 — Base Case (1-bit):** For $n = 1$, the only Gray code sequence is $\{0, 1\}$, identical to binary. The MSB rule holds trivially.

**Step 2 — Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume an $(n-1)$-bit Gray code $G^{(n-1)}$ is built from binary $B^{(n-1)}$.

**Step 3 — Reflection:** To build the $n$-bit code:
* Write $G^{(n-1)}$ forward and prefix a $0$. This gives code words $0G^{(n-1)}_k$.
* Write $G^{(n-1)}$ in reverse and prefix a $1$. This gives code words $1G^{(n-1)}_{2^{n-1}-1-k}$.

**Step 4 — Bit-by-bit check:** Consider two adjacent words in the new code, say the last of the upper half ($0G^{(n-1)}_{2^{n-1}-1}$) and the first of the lower half ($1G^{(n-1)}_{2^{n-1}-1}$). The new MSB flips (0 → 1) but the rest of the bits are *identical*, so **only one bit changes** — the cyclic property is preserved.

**Step 5 — XOR derivation:** Examining the lower halves: a binary increment from $011\ldots1$ to $100\ldots0$ flips a contiguous chain of trailing 1's and then the next 0. The XOR of consecutive bits $b_{i+1} \oplus b_i$ produces a 1 *exactly* at the boundary of the chain — yielding a single-bit Gray transition. Therefore:

$$
g_i \;=\; b_{i+1} \oplus b_i
$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Binary to Gray (KTU Standard 4-bit problem)

**Problem:** Convert binary $B = 1011$ to Gray code.

**Step 1 — List bits with indices** (MSB on the left, index $3$ down to $0$):

| Index $i$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $b_i$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Apply MSB rule** $g_3 = b_3$:

$$
g_3 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply XOR rule** $g_i = b_{i+1} \oplus b_i$ for $i = 2, 1, 0$:

$$
g_2 = b_3 \oplus b_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

$$
g_1 = b_2 \oplus b_1 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

$$
g_0 = b_1 \oplus b_0 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the result:**

$$
G = g_3 g_2 g_1 g_0 = 1110
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by K-map adjacency:** In binary, $1011$ neighbors are $1010$ (bit 0) and $0011$ (bit 3) and $1111$ (bit 2). In Gray $1110$, only one bit changes to any neighbor — the unit-distance property holds.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Gray to Binary (KTU Standard 4-bit problem)

**Problem:** Convert Gray $G = 1110$ back to binary $B$.

**Step 1 — Apply MSB rule** $b_3 = g_3$:

$$
b_3 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Apply recursive rule** $b_i = g_i \oplus b_{i+1}$ for $i = 2, 1, 0$:

$$
b_2 = g_2 \oplus b_3 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

$$
b_1 = g_1 \oplus b_2 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

$$
b_0 = g_0 \oplus b_1 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble the result:**

$$
B = 1011
$$

This matches the original binary, confirming the round-trip conversion. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.4 Complete 4-bit Reflected Gray Code Reference Table

| Decimal | Binary ($B_3 B_2 B_1 B_0$) | Gray ($G_3 G_2 G_1 G_0$) | Bits Changed from Previous Row |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0000 | 0000 | — |
| 1 | 0001 | 0001 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 2 | 0010 | 0011 | 1 (bit 1) |
| 3 | 0011 | 0010 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 4 | 0100 | 0110 | 1 (bit 2) |
| 5 | 0101 | 0111 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 6 | 0110 | 0101 | 1 (bit 1) |
| 7 | 0111 | 0100 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 8 | 1000 | 1100 | 1 (bit 3) |
| 9 | 1001 | 1101 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 10 | 1010 | 1111 | 1 (bit 1) |
| 11 | 1011 | 1110 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 12 | 1100 | 1010 | 1 (bit 2) |
| 13 | 1101 | 1011 | 1 (bit 0) |
| 14 | 1110 | 1001 | 1 (bit 1) |
| 15 | 1111 | 1000 | 1 (bit 0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observe:** Row 7 (Gray `0100`) → Row 8 (Gray `1100`) shows only the **MSB** flipping, confirming the cyclic property. Compare with binary: Row 7 `0111` → Row 8 `1000` flips *all 4 bits* simultaneously — exactly the failure mode Gray code is designed to prevent.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gray Code Conversion

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the hardware/logic flow of the two conversion operations and their shared XOR-based architecture. It maps the data path rather than depicting a physical circuit board.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Binary Input B n-1 to B 0"] --> B["MSB Pass-Through G n-1 equals B n-1"]
    B --> C["XOR Stage 1: b n-1 XOR b n-2 yields g n-2"]
    C --> D["XOR Stage 2: b n-2 XOR b n-1 yields g n-1"]
    D --> E["XOR Stage k: b 1 XOR b 0 yields g 0"]
    E --> F["Gray Output G n-1 to G 0"]

    G["Gray Input G n-1 to G 0"] --> H["MSB Pass-Through b n-1 equals g n-1"]
    H --> I["Recursive XOR 1: g n-2 XOR b n-1 yields b n-2"]
    I --> J["Recursive XOR 2: g n-3 XOR b n-2 yields b n-3"]
    J --> K["Recursive XOR m: g 0 XOR b 1 yields b 0"]
    K --> L["Binary Output B n-1 to B 0"]

    F -.Feedback Path.-> G
    L -.Inverse Path.-> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guide for the diagram:**
> * The **top branch** (A → F) is the **Binary → Gray** path; it uses a *parallel* XOR lattice.
> * The **bottom branch** (G → L) is the **Gray → Binary** path; it uses a *serial/recursive* XOR chain because each output bit depends on the previously computed bit.
> * The dashed feedback arrows represent the **mathematical invertibility** of the two transforms — applying one after the other returns the original word.

## 4.2 K-Map Adjacency Schematic (Why Gray is Used in K-Maps)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m0["m0  Gray 00"] --- m1["m1  Gray 01"]
    m1 --- m3["m3  Gray 11"]
    m3 --- m2["m2  Gray 10"]
    m2 --- m0

    m4["m4  Gray 10"] --- m5["m5  Gray 11"]
    m5 --- m7["m7  Gray 10 bottom"]
    m7 --- m6["m6  Gray 11 bottom"]
    m6 --- m4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above mesh shows how 2-bit Gray code labels wrap around a K-Map. The **wraparound edges** (m2 ↔ m0 and m6 ↔ m4) reflect the **cyclic** property — adjacent cells on a K-Map are *always* unit-distance in Gray, even across the boundary. This is why simplifying Boolean functions via K-Map grouping works: only one variable changes per cell move.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

### Q1. Define Gray code. List any two of its properties. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gray code** is a non-weighted, cyclic, unit-distance binary code in which **only one bit changes** between any two successive code words.
>
> **Properties (any two):**
> 1. It is a **non-weighted** code — bits have no fixed positional weight.
> 2. It is **cyclic** — the first and last code words also differ by exactly one bit.
> 3. It is a **unit-distance** code — Hamming distance between adjacent code words is 1.
> 4. It is constructed using the **reflect-and-prefix** method.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

### Q2. State the rule for converting a binary number to its equivalent Gray code. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Rule (Binary → Gray):**
> 1. **MSB is preserved:** The most significant bit of the Gray code is identical to the MSB of the binary number, i.e., $g_{n-1} = b_{n-1}$.
> 2. **XOR each adjacent pair:** Each subsequent Gray bit is the XOR of the corresponding binary bit and the binary bit immediately to its left (higher significance):
>
> $$
> g_i = b_{i+1} \oplus b_i \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-2
> $$
>
> **Example:** Binary $1011$ → MSB kept as $1$; then $1\oplus0=1$, $0\oplus1=1$, $1\oplus1=0$ → Gray $1110$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the construction of a 4-bit reflected Gray code using the mirror-and-prefix method. Show the complete code table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Convert the binary number $1101101$ into its equivalent Gray code using the XOR method. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mirror-and-Prefix Construction (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Start with 1-bit Gray code:** $\{0, 1\}$.

**Step 2 — Reflect and prefix for 2 bits:**
* Upper half (prefix `0`): `00, 01`
* Lower half (prefix `1`, reversed): `11, 10`
* Result: $00, 01, 11, 10$

**Step 3 — Reflect and prefix for 3 bits:**
* Upper half (prefix `0`): `000, 001, 011, 010`
* Lower half (prefix `1`, reversed): `110, 111, 101, 100`
* Result: 8 code words.

**Step 4 — Reflect and prefix for 4 bits:**
* Upper half (prefix `0`): 8 words from Step 3
* Lower half (prefix `1`, reversed): 8 words from Step 3 reversed

**Complete 4-bit Gray Table:**

| Decimal | Gray Code | Decimal | Gray Code |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0000 | 8 | 1100 |
| 1 | 0001 | 9 | 1101 |
| 2 | 0011 | 10 | 1111 |
| 3 | 0010 | 11 | 1110 |
| 4 | 0110 | 12 | 1010 |
| 5 | 0111 | 13 | 1011 |
| 6 | 0101 | 14 | 1001 |
| 7 | 0100 | 15 | 1000 |

**Verification:** Each row → next row differs in exactly 1 bit. Last (1000) → First (0000) also differs in 1 bit. $\checkmark$ (Cyclic.)

---

**(b) Binary $1101101$ → Gray (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — List binary bits:**

| Index $i$ | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $b_i$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

**Step 2 — MSB:** $g_6 = b_6 = 1$

**Step 3 — XOR each adjacent pair:**

$$
g_5 = b_6 \oplus b_5 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

$$
g_4 = b_5 \oplus b_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

$$
g_3 = b_4 \oplus b_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

$$
g_2 = b_3 \oplus b_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

$$
g_1 = b_2 \oplus b_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

$$
g_0 = b_1 \oplus b_0 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Final Gray code:**

$$
G = 1011011
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to convert a Gray code number back to its equivalent binary number. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Convert the Gray code $1011011$ into its binary equivalent. Verify by converting back. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Gray → Binary Procedure (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — MSB rule:** The MSB of the binary number equals the MSB of the Gray code: $b_{n-1} = g_{n-1}$.

**Step 2 — Recursive XOR rule:** For every subsequent bit, the binary bit is the XOR of the *current Gray bit* and the *previously computed binary bit* (to its left):

$$
b_i = g_i \oplus b_{i+1} \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \ldots, n-2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key conceptual difference:** In Binary→Gray, you XOR the binary with itself (a *parallel* operation). In Gray→Binary, you XOR the Gray with the **already-computed binary bit on the left** (a *recursive / serial* operation). This is the most common error students make in KTU exams.

**Step 3 — Hardware implication:** A parallel XOR lattice suffices for B→G; G→B requires either a serial XOR chain or a parallel-prefix tree.

---

**(b) Gray $1011011$ → Binary (7 Marks):**

| Index $i$ | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $g_i$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 1:** $b_6 = g_6 = 1$

**Step 2 — Recursive XOR:**

$$
b_5 = g_5 \oplus b_6 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

$$
b_4 = g_4 \oplus b_5 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

$$
b_3 = g_3 \oplus b_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

$$
b_2 = g_2 \oplus b_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

$$
b_1 = g_1 \oplus b_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

$$
b_0 = g_0 \oplus b_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Result:** Binary $= 1101101$.

**Step 4 — Verification (round-trip):** From Question A(b), $1101101$ (binary) → $1011011$ (Gray). We have just shown $1011011$ (Gray) → $1101101$ (binary). **Round-trip verified.** $\checkmark$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up the XOR operands in Gray→Binary:** Students often compute $b_i = g_i \oplus g_{i+1}$ (incorrect). The correct formula is $b_i = g_i \oplus b_{i+1}$ — XOR with the *already-computed binary bit to the left*, not the next Gray bit. This single mistake costs **2–3 marks** consistently.
> 2. **Forgetting the MSB rule:** A surprising number of students begin XOR from index 0 without first copying the MSB. This yields a numerically wrong answer. Always write MSB first.
> 3. **Wrong bit indexing:** Indexing bits from right-to-left as $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ is correct, but mixing up "next bit" vs. "previous bit" is a common slip. Draw a small index table before starting the calculation — a habit valued by KTU examiners.
> 4. **Assuming Gray code is weighted:** It is *not*. Do not attempt to assign positional weights $2^k$ to Gray bits. This is a **conceptual mark-loser** in 3-mark sub-questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* **Definition:** Gray code is a **non-weighted, cyclic, unit-distance** code — exactly **one bit changes** between successive code words.
* **MSB Rule (universal):** In both directions, the MSB is copied unchanged: $g_{n-1} = b_{n-1}$ and $b_{n-1} = g_{n-1}$.
* **Binary → Gray:** $g_i = b_{i+1} \oplus b_i$ — XOR of *two binary bits*. **Parallel operation.**
* **Gray → Binary:** $b_i = g_i \oplus b_{i+1}$ — XOR of *one Gray bit and the previously-computed binary bit*. **Recursive/serial operation.**
* **Cyclic Property:** First and last code words also differ in 1 bit. Use this as a quick sanity check.
* **Construction Method:** Mirror-and-prefix (reflect $(n-1)$-bit code, prepend 0 to the original, prepend 1 to the reversed copy).
* **Applications:** K-Map cell labeling, rotary/shaft encoders, asynchronous FIFO pointers, flash ADCs.
* **Not weighted:** Do **not** try to find a decimal value by $2^k$ weighting — it does not apply.
* **Unique?** No — the *reflected* Gray code is canonical, but other valid Gray codes exist.
* **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the **bit index table** before conversion; always state the **MSB rule** explicitly; always perform a **1-step verification** (round-trip convert to confirm).
* **Common Confusion:** $\oplus$ (XOR) is *not* the same as OR ($\vert$) or AND ($\&$). The XOR truth table is: $0\oplus 0=0$, $0\oplus 1=1$, $1\oplus 0=1$, $1\oplus 1=0$.
* **High-Yield Fact:** For a 4-bit system, there are $2^4 = 16$ Gray code words; the complete table is the **most frequently expected** drawing in KTU Module 1 questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Excess 3 Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT402 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Excess 3 Codes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Excess-3 Code (XS3)** is a **4-bit self-complementing non-weighted code** used to represent decimal digits. It is obtained by adding the binary value **0011 (i.e., decimal 3)** to the standard **8421 (BCD)** representation of each decimal digit. Formally, if a decimal digit $D \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 9\}$ has an 8421 (BCD) representation of $(b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0)_2$, then its Excess-3 equivalent is: $$ \text{XS3}(D) = (b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0)_2 + (0011)_2 $$ Because the code uses 4 bits per decimal digit, there are $2^4 = \mathbf{16}$ possible combinations per decade, of which **6 codes are unused** (the binary values **1010 through 1111**, i.e., decimal 10–15). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a clock that doesn't start at **0**, but at **3**. Instead of marking hours as `0, 1, 2, 3, 4...`, the clock marks them as `3, 4, 5, 6, 7...` — every number is **shifted by 3**. That is exactly what Excess-3 does to the ordinary BCD code. We "shift" every 4-bit BCD value upward by **0011 (3)**. So: - Decimal **0** (BCD: `0000`) becomes Excess-3 of `0011`. - Decimal **5** (BCD: `0101`) becomes Excess-3 of `1000`. - Decimal **9** (BCD: `1001`) becomes Excess-3 of `1100`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – KTU 2024):** The single most important property of Excess-3 you must master is the **Self-Complementing Property**. It means the 9's complement of any decimal digit can be obtained simply by **inverting every bit** of its Excess-3 representation — no arithmetic required. This dramatically simplifies subtractor hardware, which is why the code is frequently examined. ### 1.3 Position in KTU Module 1 Roadmap In Module 1, codes are usually introduced in this order: 1. **8421 / BCD** (weighted) 2. **Excess-3 (XS3)** (non-weighted, self-complementing) ← *You are here* 3. **2421, 5211, 84-2-1** (other self-complementing weighted codes) 4. **Gray Code, ASCII** (error detection / alphanumeric) Understanding **why** XS3 is "self-complementing" before moving to 2421 will make the rest of the module intuitive. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Excess-3 — Motivation Standard BCD has a serious drawback: its **9's complement cannot be obtained by bit inversion** alone. - Decimal 3 → BCD `0011` - Decimal 6 (9's complement of 3) → BCD `0110` - Inverting `0011` gives `1100` (BCD for **12**, NOT 6). ❌ Excess-3 fixes this beautifully. By adding 3 to every digit, we "center" the codes symmetrically around the middle of the 4-bit range. The midpoint of a 4-bit code is between `0111` (7) and `1000` (8) — i.e., at value **7.5**. Excess-3's unused range (0000–0010 and 1101–1111) creates a natural **symmetry buffer** that makes every code's bitwise inverse land on another valid code. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** XS3 codes are *always* ≥ `0011` and *always* ≤ `1100`. Anything outside this band is **invalid** in XS3 and can be used as **error-detection redundancy**. ### 2.2 Construction Rules — The Three Conversion Rules **Rule 1 — BCD → Excess-3 (Encoding):** Add binary `0011` to the 4-bit BCD digit. $$ \text{XS3} = \text{BCD} + 0011 $$ **Rule 2 — Excess-3 → BCD (Decoding):** Subtract binary `0011` from the 4-bit XS3 digit. $$ \text{BCD} = \text{XS3} - 0011 $$ **Rule 3 — 9's Complement via Bit Inversion (Self-Complementing):** $$ \text{XS3}(9 - D) = \overline{\text{XS3}(D)} $$ where the overline denotes **bitwise NOT** (invert every bit). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Operation | Formula / Rule | Result Type | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Encode BCD → XS3 | $\text{XS3} = \text{BCD} + 0011_2$ | 4-bit non-weighted | $5 (0101) \rightarrow 1000$ | | Decode XS3 → BCD | $\text{BCD} = \text{XS3} - 0011_2$ | 4-bit weighted | $1000 \rightarrow 5 (0101)$ | | 9's complement | $\text{XS3}(\bar{D}) = \overline{\text{XS3}(D)}$ | 4-bit (bit-inverted) | $\text{XS3}(3) = 0110 \rightarrow \text{XS3}(6) = 1001$ | | Validity Range | $0011 \le \text{XS3} \le 1100$ | Boolean test | `0000`, `1101`, `1110`, `1111` are **invalid** | | Unused codes per decade | $2^4 - 10 = 6$ | Count | `1010`, `1011`, `1101`, `1110`, `1111`, and `0000`–`0010` | | Group weights (one view) | $8, 4, -2, -1$ (signed weights) | Signed-decimal | $1100 = 8+4-2-1 = 9$ ✔ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipes inside tables** — note the use of "$\le$" and "$\ge$" symbols rather than $\vert \dots \vert$ to keep the markdown table parser happy. When writing your answer script, use **$\le$** in LaTeX form. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Early decimal arithmetic units (e.g., IBM 1401, 1960s mainframes)** used XS3 internally because the **9's complement = bit-inversion** property meant a subtractor could be built from a single bank of NOT gates — no full adder needed. - **Modern barcode/UPC code systems** in some legacy point-of-sale hardware still rely on XS3 for parity-style validation. - **BCD arithmetic ICs** (like the classic **TTL 74LS283** cascaded with a +3 offset network) used XS3 internally to simplify decimal subtraction. - It is a classic **interview question** for VLSI/digital design roles — expect it in placement tests. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Decimal-to-XS3 Conversion Table (Analytical Matrix) This is the **single most-memorized table** for KTU Module 1 — reproduce it cold in the exam hall. | Decimal Digit | 8421 (BCD) | + 0011 (Binary Add) | **Excess-3 (XS3)** | 9's Comp. Dec | XS3 of 9's Comp. | Inverted XS3 (Verify) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0000 | 0000 + 0011 | **0011** | 9 | 1100 | ¬0011 = 1100 ✔ | | 1 | 0001 | 0001 + 0011 | **0100** | 8 | 1011 | ¬0100 = 1011 ✔ | | 2 | 0010 | 0010 + 0011 | **0101** | 7 | 1010 | ¬0101 = 1010 ✔ | | 3 | 0011 | 0011 + 0011 | **0110** | 6 | 1001 | ¬0110 = 1001 ✔ | | 4 | 0100 | 0100 + 0011 | **0111** | 5 | 1000 | ¬0111 = 1000 ✔ | | 5 | 0101 | 0101 + 0011 | **1000** | 4 | 0111 | ¬1000 = 0111 ✔ | | 6 | 0110 | 0110 + 0011 | **1001** | 3 | 0110 | ¬1001 = 0110 ✔ | | 7 | 0111 | 0111 + 0011 | **1010** | 2 | 0101 | ¬1010 = 0101 ✔ | | 8 | 1000 | 1000 + 0011 | **1011** | 1 | 0100 | ¬1011 = 0100 ✔ | | 9 | 1001 | 1001 + 0011 | **1100** | 0 | 0011 | ¬1100 = 0011 ✔ | > The "Verify" column proves the self-complementing property for **every single digit** — this is the proof a 14-mark question will demand. ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Encoding the Decimal Number 472 **Problem:** Convert $(472)_{10}$ to Excess-3 code, with all working shown. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Split decimal into BCD digits} \quad & 4 \;\; 7 \;\; 2 \\ \text{Step 2: Write each digit in 8421 (BCD)} \quad & 0100 \;\; 0111 \;\; 0010 \\ \text{Step 3: Add } 0011 \text{ to each 4-bit group} \quad & \\ \quad 0100 + 0011 &= 0111 \quad (\text{carry chain shown below}) \\ \quad 0111 + 0011 &= 1010 \\ \quad 0010 + 0011 &= 0101 \\ \text{Step 4: Concatenate the XS3 nibbles} \quad & \mathbf{0111\;1010\;0101} \end{aligned} $$ **Detailed bit-add for the first nibble ($0100 + 0011$):** $$ \begin{aligned} 0 + 0 &= 0 \quad (\text{bit 0, LSB}) \\ 0 + 1 &= 1 \quad (\text{bit 1}) \\ 1 + 1 &= 0, \; c = 1 \quad (\text{bit 2, plus carry}) \\ 0 + 0 + 1 &= 1 \quad (\text{bit 3, MSB}) \\ \therefore \text{Result} &= 0111 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $(472)_{10} = (0111\;1010\;0101)_{\text{XS3}}$ ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Decoding XS3 Back to Decimal **Problem:** Decode $(1100\;0110\;1001)_{\text{XS3}}$ to decimal. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Nibble 1: } 1100 - 0011 &= 1001 = 9 \\ \text{Nibble 2: } 0110 - 0011 &= 0011 = 3 \\ \text{Nibble 3: } 1001 - 0011 &= 0110 = 6 \\ \text{Concatenate decimal digits: } & 936 \end{aligned} $$ **Detailed bit-sub for $1100 - 0011$:** $$ \begin{aligned} 0 - 1 &: \text{borrow, } 2 - 1 = 1, \; b = 1 \quad (\text{bit 0}) \\ 0 - 1 - 1 &: \text{borrow, } 2 - 1 - 1 = 0, \; b = 1 \quad (\text{bit 1}) \\ 1 - 0 - 1 &= 0, \; b = 0 \quad (\text{bit 2}) \\ 1 - 0 - 0 &= 1 \quad (\text{bit 3}) \\ \therefore \text{Result} &= 1001 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $(1100\;0110\;1001)_{\text{XS3}} = (936)_{10}$ ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — 9's Complement via Bit Inversion (The Star Question) **Problem:** Find the 9's complement of decimal **428** using Excess-3 self-complementing property. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Convert 428 to XS3} \quad & \\ \quad 4 \rightarrow 0111, \;\; 2 \rightarrow 0101, \;\; 8 \rightarrow 1011 & \\ \quad \text{XS3}(428) = 0111\;0101\;1011 & \\ \text{Step 2: Bitwise invert every bit} \quad & \\ \quad \overline{0111} = 1000, \;\; \overline{0101} = 1010, \;\; \overline{1011} = 0100 & \\ \text{Step 3: Concatenate the inverted nibbles} \quad & \mathbf{1000\;1010\;0100} \\ \text{Step 4: Verify by direct decimal method} \quad & \\ \quad 9 - 4 = 5, \;\; 9 - 2 = 7, \;\; 9 - 8 = 1 & \\ \quad \text{Expected: } 571 & \\ \text{Step 5: Decode our XS3 result to confirm} \quad & \\ \quad 1000 - 0011 = 0101 = 5 & \\ \quad 1010 - 0011 = 0111 = 7 & \\ \quad 0100 - 0011 = 0001 = 1 & \\ \text{Confirmed: } 571 \;\; \checkmark & \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — XS3 Addition (with carry correction) **Problem:** Add $(24)_{10} + (18)_{10}$ using Excess-3 arithmetic. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Convert each operand to XS3} \quad & \\ \text{XS3}(24) = 0101\;0111 & \\ \text{XS3}(18) = 0100\;1011 & \\ \text{Step 2: Perform nibble-wise 4-bit binary addition} \quad & \\ \quad 0111 + 1011 &= 1\;0010 \quad (\text{carry} = 1, \text{ sum} = 0010) \\ \quad 0101 + 0100 + 1 &= 1010 \quad (\text{cumulative carry-out} = 0) & \\ \text{Step 3: Apply XS3 carry correction — add } 0011 \text{ to each non-carry nibble} \quad & \\ \text{Carry-out } 1 \text{ means the LSB nibble produced an "excess" of 16.} \quad & \\ \text{We propagate this: add } 0011 \text{ to the LSB nibble that generated the carry.} \quad & \\ \quad 0010 + 0011 &= 0101 \\ \text{MSB nibble had no carry-out, so no correction needed there.} \quad & \\ \text{Step 4: Final XS3 sum} \quad & \mathbf{1010\;0101} \\ \text{Step 5: Decode: } 1010 = 7, \; 0101 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (42)_{10} & \; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **XS3 Addition Rule (Frequently Asked in KTU):** > 1. Add the XS3 numbers nibble-by-nibble using standard binary addition. > 2. If a 4-bit addition produces a **carry-out**, add binary `0011` to that nibble's sum. > 3. If a 4-bit addition does **NOT** produce a carry-out, **subtract** binary `0011` from that nibble's sum. > 4. If a final carry-out emerges from the MSB nibble, prepend an extra `0011` nibble to the result. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Excess-3 Encoding/Decoding Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Decimal
Digit D"] --> B["BCD Encoder
8421 Lookup"] B --> C["4-bit BCD
b3 b2 b1 b0"] C --> D["+ 0011
Binary Adder"] D --> E["4-bit XS3
x3 x2 x1 x0"] E --> F["Inverter Bank
NOT gates"] F --> G["XS3 of 9-D
9s Complement"] E --> H["- 0011
Binary Subtractor"] H --> I["4-bit BCD
Decoded Digit"] ``` ### 4.2 State Transition — Self-Complementing Symmetry ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["Lower Half of Valid XS3 Codes (0 to 4)"] X0["XS3 of 0
0011"] X1["XS3 of 1
0100"] X2["XS3 of 2
0101"] X3["XS3 of 3
0110"] X4["XS3 of 4
0111"] end subgraph S2["Upper Half of Valid XS3 Codes (5 to 9)"] Y5["XS3 of 5
1000"] Y6["XS3 of 6
1001"] Y7["XS3 of 7
1010"] Y8["XS3 of 8
1011"] Y9["XS3 of 9
1100"] end X0 -.bit invert.-> Y9 X1 -.bit invert.-> Y8 X2 -.bit invert.-> Y7 X3 -.bit invert.-> Y6 X4 -.bit invert.-> Y5 ``` > The dotted arrows above prove the self-complementing property: inverting every bit of any XS3 code produces the XS3 code of its **9's complement** digit. ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of an XS3-Based Decimal Subtractor ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Decimal Minuend
M"] --> B["XS3 Encoder
+ 0011"] C["Decimal Subtrahend
N"] --> D["XS3 Encoder
+ 0011"] D --> E["Bit Inverter
NOT gates"] E --> F["9s Complement of N
in XS3 form"] B --> G["4-bit Binary
Adder"] F --> G G --> H["End-Around
Carry Logic"] H --> I["XS3 Result"] I --> J["XS3 Decoder
- 0011"] J --> K["Decimal
Difference M - N"] ``` > This is the **canonical KTU diagram** for showing why Excess-3 simplifies decimal subtraction hardware: the 9's complement is "free" (just inverters), and the addition uses the same 4-bit binary adder that handles other arithmetic. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)** #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **What is Excess-3 code? Mention any two of its properties.** **Model Answer:** Excess-3 (XS3) code is a 4-bit non-weighted code obtained by adding binary `0011` (decimal 3) to the standard 8421 BCD representation of a decimal digit. **Two key properties:** 1. **Self-complementing property:** The 9's complement of a decimal digit is obtained by simply inverting every bit of its Excess-3 code. 2. **Non-weighted code:** Unlike BCD, the bit positions in XS3 do not carry fixed weights like 8, 4, 2, 1 (although a signed-weight representation of $8, 4, -2, -1$ exists). 3. *Bonus:* There are 6 unused codes per decade (`0000`–`0010` and `1101`–`1111`), which aid in error detection. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **State the self-complementing property of Excess-3 code. Verify it for the decimal digit 6.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** The 9's complement of any decimal digit, when represented in Excess-3 code, is equal to the bitwise complement (logical inversion) of the original digit's Excess-3 code. **Verification for decimal 6:** - Excess-3 representation of **6** is `1001`. - 9's complement of decimal 6 is decimal **3**. - Excess-3 representation of **3** is `0110`. - Bitwise complement of `1001` is `0110`. ✔ **Verified.** --- ### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)** > **ESE Module Choice Convention:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both choices are of equal difficulty and carry the same mark distribution. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Excess-3 code in detail. Construct the complete Excess-3 code table for all decimal digits 0 to 9 and explain the self-complementing property using one example. **(b) [7 Marks)** Convert the following numbers from the given base to the requested base, showing all working: 1. $(385)_{10}$ → Excess-3 2. $(1001\;0100\;0110)_{\text{XS3}}$ → Decimal 3. Find the 9's complement of $(752)_{10}$ using the self-complementing property of Excess-3. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Detailed Explanation and Table:** [See Section 3.1 — the analytical matrix above. Present that exact table in your answer script, with column headings "Decimal, 8421 BCD, XS3 Code".] **Self-complementing example:** (Pick decimal 2) - XS3(2) = `0101` - 9's complement of 2 = 7 - XS3(7) = `1010` - Bitwise NOT of `0101` is `1010` ✔ The property holds because Excess-3's "midpoint" lies exactly at 7.5, and codes lie symmetrically around it. --- **(b) Conversions:** **1. $(385)_{10}$ → XS3** $$ \begin{aligned} 3 \rightarrow 0011 + 0011 &= 0110 \\ 8 \rightarrow 1000 + 0011 &= 1011 \\ 5 \rightarrow 0101 + 0011 &= 1000 \\ \therefore (385)_{10} &= \mathbf{(0110\;1011\;1000)_{\text{XS3}}} \end{aligned} $$ **2. $(1001\;0100\;0110)_{\text{XS3}}$ → Decimal** $$ \begin{aligned} 1001 - 0011 &= 0110 = 6 \\ 0100 - 0011 &= 0001 = 1 \\ 0110 - 0011 &= 0011 = 3 \\ \therefore \text{Result} &= \mathbf{(613)_{10}} \end{aligned} $$ **3. 9's complement of $(752)_{10}$ using XS3** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: XS3}(752) &= 1010\;1000\;0101 \\ \text{Step 2: Bit-invert each nibble} &= 0101\;0111\;1010 \\ \text{Step 3: Decode} \quad 0101 &= 5,\; 0111 = 7,\; 1010 = 7 \\ \therefore \text{9's complement of 752} &= \mathbf{(247)_{10}} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification (direct method):** $9-7=2$, $9-5=4$, $9-2=7$ → reads as `247` reversed as `247` from LSD to MSD. ✔ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Excess-3 code with 8421 BCD code. List any four differences and explain with one example why Excess-3 is preferred for decimal subtraction circuits. **(b) [7 Marks]** Perform the following Excess-3 arithmetic and verify the result by converting back to decimal: 1. $(47)_{10} + (28)_{10}$ 2. $(91)_{10} - (36)_{10}$ (using the self-complementing property to perform the subtraction) --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Comparison Table:** | Feature | 8421 (BCD) | Excess-3 (XS3) | |---|---|---| | Type | Weighted ($8,4,2,1$) | Non-weighted (or signed weights $8,4,-2,-1$) | | Generation | Direct binary | Add `0011` to BCD | | 9's complement | Not bit-invertible | Self-complementing (bit-invertible) | | Unused codes | 6 (`1010`–`1111`) | 6 (`0000`–`0010`, `1101`–`1111`) | | Hardware for subtractor | Needs full 9's-comp adder + end-around carry | Needs only NOT gates + same adder | | First code | `0000` (for 0) | `0011` (for 0) | | Last code | `1001` (for 9) | `1100` (for 9) | | Error detection | Limited | Better — symmetric range | **Why XS3 is preferred for decimal subtraction (example):** To compute $7 - 4$ in decimal: - **With BCD:** Compute 9's complement of 4 → 9-4 = 5 (requires decimal subtract logic), then add 7 + 5 = 12, then add the end-around carry 1 → result 3. - **With XS3:** Convert 7 → `1010`, Convert 4 → `0111`. Bit-invert 4's XS3 → `1000` (which is XS3 of 5, the 9's comp). Add `1010 + 1000 = 1 0010`. Add carry correction `0010 + 0011 = 0101` (XS3 of 2). Add 1 from carry to MSB nibble `0000 + 1 + 0011 = 0100` (XS3 of 1). Final: `0100 0101` → decode to `(13)$_{10}$`. ✔ The XS3 version uses **only an adder and NOT gates**, whereas BCD needs an extra decimal-complement subtractor block. --- **(b) Excess-3 Arithmetic:** **1. $(47)_{10} + (28)_{10}$** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{XS3}(47) &= 0111\;1010 \\ \text{XS3}(28) &= 0101\;1011 \\ \text{Add nibble-wise:} & \\ \quad 1010 + 1011 &= 1\;0101 \quad (\text{carry-out} = 1) \\ \quad 0111 + 0101 + 1 &= 1101 \quad (\text{no carry-out}) \\ \text{Apply correction rule:} & \\ \quad \text{LSB produced carry} \Rightarrow 0101 + 0011 &= 1000 \\ \quad \text{MSB no carry} \Rightarrow 1101 - 0011 &= 1010 \\ \text{Result: } 1010\;1000 & \\ \text{Decode: } 1010 &= 7,\; 1000 = 5 \\ \therefore \text{Sum} = \mathbf{(75)_{10}} & \;\; \checkmark \quad (47 + 28 = 75) \end{aligned} $$ **2. $(91)_{10} - (36)_{10}$ via self-complementing** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{XS3}(91) &= 1100\;0100 \\ \text{XS3}(36) &= 0110\;1001 \\ \text{Bit-invert 36's XS3 to get 9's comp of 36 in XS3 form:} & \\ \quad \overline{0110\;1001} &= 1001\;0110 \quad (\text{this is XS3 of 63}) \\ \text{Add to XS3}(91): & \\ \quad 1100 + 1001 &= 1\;0101 \quad (\text{carry-out} = 1) \\ \quad 0100 + 0110 + 1 &= 1011 \quad (\text{no carry-out}) \\ \text{Apply correction:} & \\ \quad 0101 + 0011 &= 1000 \\ \quad 1011 - 0011 &= 1000 \\ \text{Add end-around carry: } 1000 + 0001 &= 1001 \;\; \text{(but in XS3 form we just add 1 to MSD, no correction needed)} \\ \text{Final XS3 result: } 1001\;1000 & \\ \text{Decode: } 1001 &= 6,\; 1000 = 5 \\ \therefore (91 - 36) = \mathbf{(55)_{10}} & \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students most commonly lose marks on Excess-3 questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to add 0011 separately to each 4-bit BCD nibble.** A common mistake is to convert the *entire decimal number* to binary first and then add `0011` to the whole 8-bit/12-bit block. **Always work nibble-by-nibble.** > 2. **Confusing XS3 with BCD in the addition step.** When adding XS3 numbers, you must apply the **carry correction rule** (add `0011` if carry-out, subtract `0011` if no carry-out). Skipping this is a guaranteed 2-3 mark loss. > 3. **Mishandling the self-complementing proof.** Examiners award marks only if the student **verifies with an example AND states the general rule** (bitwise inversion gives 9's complement). Merely stating the property without proof = 0 marks. > 4. **Forgetting the 6 unused codes.** Examiners often ask "Why is XS3 useful for error detection?" The answer lies in the **unused code space** — any appearance of `0000`, `0001`, `0010`, `1101`, `1110`, or `1111` indicates a transmission error. > 5. **Decimal 0 problem.** Some students write XS3(0) = `0000` instead of `0011`. Memorize: **XS3(0) = 0011**, NOT `0000`. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** - ✅ **Definition:** XS3 = BCD + `0011` (per nibble). - ✅ **Bits used:** 4 bits per decimal digit; **6 codes unused** per decade. - ✅ **Type:** **Non-weighted**, **self-complementing** code. - ✅ **Range:** Valid codes lie in `[0011, 1100]` only. - ✅ **Self-complementing rule:** 9's complement of decimal = bitwise inversion of XS3. - ✅ **Encoding (BCD → XS3):** Add `0011` to each BCD nibble. - ✅ **Decoding (XS3 → BCD):** Subtract `0011` from each XS3 nibble. - ✅ **Addition rule:** After nibble-wise binary addition, add `0011` if carry-out, subtract `0011` if no carry-out. - ✅ **Subtraction trick:** Bit-invert the subtrahend's XS3 form (gives 9's complement) and add. - ✅ **Error detection:** Codes `0000`, `0001`, `0010`, `1101`, `1110`, `1111` are **forbidden** in valid XS3 transmissions. - ✅ **Why it's taught in KTU:** Bridges the conceptual gap between **weighted BCD** and **arithmetic-friendly non-weighted codes** like 2421 that follow in Module 1. - ✅ **Memory anchor for exam:** *"Three above BCD, mirror-image complement."*

topics.title: Basics of Verilog – Basic Language Elements: Identifiers, Data Objects, Scalar Data Types, Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT402 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Verilog – Basic Language Elements: Identifiers, Data Objects, Scalar Data Types, Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Verilog HDL?
**Verilog (Verifying Logic)** is a **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** standardized as **IEEE 1364**. It is used to model, simulate, and synthesize digital electronic systems at multiple levels of abstraction — behavioral, data-flow, gate-level, and switch-level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 focuses on the **lexical foundation** of Verilog. You are NOT learning how to design circuits yet — you are learning the **grammar and vocabulary** (identifiers, data objects, types, operators) that every later Verilog construct will use.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Verilog as a Recipe"
Think of Verilog as a **recipe for a chef**:
- **Identifiers** → Names of ingredients (flour, sugar, eggs)
- **Data Objects** → The bowls/containers that hold ingredients (`wire bowl1`, `reg bowl2`)
- **Data Types** → The *kind* of ingredient (liquid, solid, powder — i.e., `0`, `1`, `x`, `z`)
- **Operators** → Cooking actions (mix, heat, cut — i.e., `&`, `|`, `+`, `~`)

Just as a chef cannot cook without knowing ingredient names, container types, and cooking actions, **a digital designer cannot write Verilog without mastering identifiers, data objects, types, and operators**.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Verilog Lexical Foundation

| Pillar | Formal Definition | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Identifiers** | User-defined names for objects, modules, ports, signals | The "labels" you stick on wires and boxes |
| **Data Objects** | Symbolic containers holding values during simulation | Buckets that store electrical states |
| **Scalar Data Types** | Single-bit values representing logic states | A single light switch (ON/OFF/unknown/floating) |
| **Operators** | Symbols performing arithmetic, logical, bitwise, or relational actions | The verbs that act on the data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Identifier (IEEE 1364 §3.7):** *"An identifier is a sequence of letters, digits, underscores (_), and dollar signs ($). The first character must be a letter or underscore — it cannot be a digit or $."*

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations
Module 1 typically contributes **15–20%** of ESE questions. Common 3-mark questions ask you to *"List the rules for identifiers"* or *"Explain the four-value logic system"*. A 14-mark question may ask you to *"Explain lexical conventions and demonstrate operator precedence with examples."* Without solid command of these basics, the later modules (gate-level modeling, dataflow modeling) become impossible to write syntactically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Identifiers — Rules and Conventions

**2.1.1 Definition:**
An **identifier** is any name used to label a Verilog object — signal, module, port, instance, function, task, block label, or event.

**2.1.2 Lexical Rules (Board-Exam Critical):**

```
1. Allowed characters   → A–Z, a–z, 0–9, underscore (_), dollar ($)
2. First character      → MUST be a letter (A–Z / a–z) or underscore (_)
3. Case sensitivity     → YES — "Output" and "output" are different
4. Maximum length       → Implementation-dependent (typically 1024 chars)
5. Reserved keywords    → CANNOT be used as identifiers
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reserved Keywords (You MUST NOT use as identifiers):** `module, input, output, wire, reg, always, assign, begin, end, if, else, case, while, for, function, task, integer, real, parameter, and, or, not, nand, nor, xor, xnor, buf, notif0, notif1, bufif0, bufif1, supply0, supply1, tri, tri0, tri1, triand, trior, wand, wor, etc.`

**2.1.3 Valid vs Invalid Identifier Examples:**

| Identifier | Valid? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `clk_100MHz` | ✅ Yes | Starts with letter, uses underscore + digits |
| `_reset_n` | ✅ Yes | Starts with underscore |
| `data$bus` | ✅ Yes | Dollar allowed (but **not** first char) |
| `8bit_bus` | ❌ No | Starts with a digit |
| `module` | ❌ No | Reserved keyword |
| `out put` | ❌ No | Space is illegal |
| `clk-frequency` | ❌ No | Hyphen is illegal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Practice (Industry Standard):** Use **snake_case** with descriptive suffixes: `_n` for active-low, `_clk` for clocks, `_r` for registered outputs. Example: `chip_select_n`, `data_in_bus[7:0]`.

---

### 2.2 Data Objects — The Three Categories

A **data object** is a symbolic container that holds a value during simulation. Verilog has **three classes** of data objects:

**2.2.1 Nets (`wire`, `wand`, `wor`, `tri`, etc.)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A `net` represents a **physical connection** between components. It does **not store** a value — its value is continuously driven by whatever is connected to it (continuous assignment, gate output, or port).

| Net Type | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `wire` | Single-driver or multi-driver resolved connection | Most common signal type |
| `wand` | Wired-AND (multi-driver, AND resolution) | Open-collector buses |
| `wor` | Wired-OR | Open-emitter buses |
| `tri` | 3-state net | Bidirectional buses |
| `tri0`, `tri1` | Pull-down / pull-up resistive net | Pull-up resistors |
| `supply0`, `supply1` | Logic 0 / 1 power rail | VSS / VDD modeling |

**2.2.2 Registers (`reg`, `integer`, `real`, `time`, `realtime`)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A `register` is a **data storage element** that holds its value until a **procedural assignment** updates it inside `always`, `initial`, `task`, or `function` blocks. The keyword `reg` does **NOT** imply a physical flip-flop — it merely means "can hold a value."

**2.2.3 Parameters (`parameter`, `localparam`)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A `parameter` is a **compile-time constant**. Unlike `wire`/`reg`, parameters cannot be reassigned at runtime. `localparam` is restricted to its containing module (cannot be overridden during instantiation).

```
parameter WIDTH = 8;
localparam MAX_COUNT = (1 << WIDTH) - 1;   // = 255
```

---

### 2.3 Scalar Data Types — The Four-Value Logic System

This is one of the **most frequently tested 3-mark concepts** in KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Four-Valued Logic (IEEE 1364 §3.1):** *"Verilog uses a 4-value logic system where every bit can take the value 0, 1, x, or z."*

| Value | Meaning | Real-World Equivalent | Strength Hierarchy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | Logic zero (false, low) | Switch OFF, 0 V | Strong 0 |
| **1** | Logic one (true, high) | Switch ON, 3.3 V/5 V | Strong 1 |
| **x** | Unknown / ambiguous | Floating probe, contention, uninitialized | **Weakest** |
| **z** | High-impedance (tri-state) | Disconnected wire, disabled output buffer | Tri-state |

**Truth-Value Resolution for Multiple Drivers on a `wire`:**

| Driver A | Driver B | Resolved `wire` |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **0** |
| 0 | 1 | **x** (contention — KTU favorite!) |
| 1 | 1 | **1** |
| 0 | z | **0** |
| 1 | z | **1** |
| x | z | **x** |
| z | z | **z** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write `wire = 0+1 = 1`. This is **WRONG** in Verilog. Two drivers forcing opposite values produce **`x`**, not `1`. Memorize the table above.

**Integer & Real Types:**
- `integer` → 32-bit signed **two's-complement** number (range: $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$)
- `real` → IEEE 754 double-precision floating point
- `time` → 64-bit unsigned (used with `$time` system function)

---

### 2.4 Operators — The Complete Classification

Verilog has **~9 operator categories** as per IEEE 1364. KTU 2024 Module 1 specifically tests the **first four** listed below.

| Category | Operators | Associativity | Operand Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Arithmetic | `+  -  *  /  %` | Left | Numeric |
| 2. Relational | `>  <  >=  <=` | Left | Numeric |
| 3. Equality | `==  !=  ===  !==` | Left | Bit-pattern |
| 4. Logical | `&&  ||  !` | Left | Boolean |
| 5. Bitwise | `~  &  |  ^  ~^` | Left | Bit-wise |
| 6. Reduction | `&  ~&  \|  ~\|  ^  ~^` | Left | Unary |
| 7. Shift | `<<  >>  <<<  >>>` | Left | Bit-wise |
| 8. Conditional | `?  :` | Right | Ternary |
| 9. Concatenation & Replication | `{  }` `{  {  }  }` | — | Bit lists |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`==` vs `===` (Board Exam Favorite!):**
> - `==` (logical equality): compares **0/1 only** — `x` and `z` match anything → returns `x`
> - `===` (case equality): compares **all four values including x and z** → always returns 0 or 1
>
> Example: `(4'b101x == 4'b1010)` evaluates to **`x`**, but `(4'b101x === 4'b101x)` evaluates to **`1`**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

**Operator Precedence (Highest → Lowest):**

| Level | Operators | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (highest) | `+  -  !  ~  &  ~&  \|  ~\|  ^  ~^  (unary)` | Highest precedence — unary operators |
| 2 | `**` | Power |
| 3 | `*  /  %` | Multiplicative |
| 4 | `+  -` | Additive |
| 5 | `<<  >>  <<<  >>>` | Shifts |
| 6 | `<  <=  >  >=` | Relational |
| 7 | `==  !=  ===  !==` | Equality |
| 8 | `&  ~&` | Bitwise AND / NAND |
| 9 | `^  ~^` | Bitwise XOR / XNOR |
| 10 | `\|  ~\|` | Bitwise OR / NOR |
| 11 | `&&` | Logical AND |
| 12 | `\|\|` | Logical OR |
| 13 (lowest) | `?:` | Conditional |

**The Master Equation — Operator Result Type:**

$$
\text{result type} =
\begin{cases}
\text{integer}, & \text{if any operand is real} \\
\text{signed}, & \text{if all operands are signed AND context is signed} \\
\text{unsigned 32-bit}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Bit-Width Self-Determining Rule:**

$$
L_{\text{expr}} = \begin{cases}
\max(L_a, L_b), & \text{for binary ops (+, -, \&, \vert, ^)} \\
L_a, & \text{for unary ops (\~, !)} \\
L_a, & \text{for reductions (\&, \vert, ^)} \\
\end{cases}
$$

where $L_x$ is the bit-width of operand $x$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These rules power **synthesis tools** like **Synopsys Design Compiler** and **Xilinx Vivado** — every FPGA and ASIC chip in the world begins as a Verilog description obeying these lexical and type rules.

---

### 2.6 Logical vs Bitwise vs Reduction — The Confusing Trio

This is the **#1 conceptual trap** for KTU students:

| Operation | Symbol | Operates On | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bitwise** | `&  \|  ^` | Each corresponding bit **independently** | `4'b1010 & 4'b1100` = `4'b1000` |
| **Logical** | `&&  \|\|` | Entire operand as **one boolean** (returns 1-bit) | `(4'b1010 && 4'b1100)` = `1'b1` |
| **Reduction** | `&  ~&  \|  ^` (unary) | **All bits of one operand** → produces 1-bit | `&4'b1010` = `1'b0` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap:** `a && b` is **NOT** the same as `a & b`. The first reduces operands to 1-bit booleans; the second performs bit-by-bit AND.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Reduction Operator on a 4-bit Vector

**Problem:** Evaluate `&4'b1010` and `|4'b1010` step-by-step.

**Solution (Reduction AND):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{operand} &= 4'b1010 \\
&= b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 \\
&= 1,\ 0,\ 1,\ 0 \\
\text{Reduction AND: } &b_3 \ \&\ b_2 \ \&\ b_1 \ \&\ b_0 \\
&= 1 \ \&\ 0 \ \&\ 1 \ \&\ 0 \\
&= 0 \ \&\ 1 \ \&\ 0 \\
&= 0 \ \&\ 0 \\
&= \mathbf{1'b0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (Reduction OR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction OR: } &b_3 \ \vert\ b_2 \ \vert\ b_1 \ \vert\ b_0 \\
&= 1 \ \vert\ 0 \ \vert\ 1 \ \vert\ 0 \\
&= 1 \ \vert\ 1 \ \vert\ 0 \\
&= 1 \ \vert\ 0 \\
&= \mathbf{1'b1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Reduction operators apply the binary operator across **all bits of a single operand from MSB to LSB**, returning exactly **1 bit**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Operator Precedence in a Complex Expression

**Problem:** Evaluate `a + b * c > d && e == f` for `a=4, b=2, c=3, d=9, e=1, f=1`.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expression:} \quad &a + b \cdot c > d\ \&\&\ e == f \\
\text{Step 1: Precedence Level 3 — Multiplication first} \quad &b \cdot c = 2 \times 3 = 6 \\
\text{Expression becomes:} \quad &a + 6 > d\ \&\&\ e == f \\
\text{Step 2: Precedence Level 4 — Addition} \quad &a + 6 = 4 + 6 = 10 \\
\text{Expression becomes:} \quad &10 > d\ \&\&\ e == f \\
\text{Step 3: Precedence Level 6 — Relational >} \quad &10 > 9 = 1\ (\text{true}) \\
\text{Expression becomes:} \quad &1\ \&\&\ e == f \\
\text{Step 4: Precedence Level 7 — Equality ==} \quad &e == f = (1 == 1) = 1 \\
\text{Expression becomes:} \quad &1\ \&\&\ 1 \\
\text{Step 5: Precedence Level 11 — Logical AND} \quad &1\ \&\&\ 1 = \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** `1`

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Bit-Width Self-Determining Rule

**Problem:** What is the bit-width of `4'b1010 + 4'b0110`? Show each step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operand widths:} \quad &L_a = 4,\ L_b = 4 \\
\text{Apply rule:} \quad &L_{\text{result}} = \max(L_a, L_b) = \max(4, 4) = 4 \\
\text{Perform operation:} \quad &4'b1010 + 4'b0110 = 4'b1010 + 4'b0110 \\
&= (1010)_2 + (0110)_2 \\
&= (10)_{10} + (6)_{10} \\
&= (16)_{10} \\
\text{Convert to 4-bit binary:} \quad &(16)_{10} = (10000)_2 \\
\text{Truncate to 4 bits:} \quad &\mathbf{4'b0000} \\
\text{Carry-out flag:} \quad &\text{overflow bit is LOST in 4-bit context} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Although $10 + 6 = 16$ requires 5 bits, Verilog **truncates** the MSB (the carry) to fit the declared context width. This is why **bit-width mismatch** is a top cause of simulation mismatches in industry.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Concatenation and Replication Operators

**Problem:** Given `a = 4'b1010`, `b = 4'b1101`, evaluate `{a, b}`, `{2{a}}`, and `{b, 4'b0000}`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{a, b\} &= \{4'b1010, 4'b1101\} = 8'b10101101 \\
\{2\{a\}\} &= \{a, a\} = \{4'b1010, 4'b1010\} = 8'b10101010 \\
\{b, 4'b0000\} &= \{4'b1101, 4'b0000\} = 8'b11010000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operator Behavior on `x` and `z`

| Operation | `x & 1` | `x & 0` | `x & x` | `z & 1` | `~x` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Result** | **`x`** | **`0`** | **`x`** | **`x`** | **`x`** |

| Operation | `x \| 1` | `x \| 0` | `x ^ 1` | `x ^ x` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Result** | **`1`** | **`x`** | **`x`** | **`x`** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** You will frequently be asked: *"What is `x & 0`?"* Answer: **0** (because AND with 0 always gives 0 regardless of the other input). But `x & 1` = **`x`** (because `x` could be 0 or 1 — we don't know).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Identifier Validity (Self-Assessment Table)

| Identifier | Verdict | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `count_8bit` | ✅ Legal | Letter start, digit/end |
| `_CS_n` | ✅ Legal | Underscore start allowed |
| `data_bus$1` | ✅ Legal | `$` allowed not-first |
| `8bit_count` | ❌ Illegal | Cannot start with digit |
| `data-bus` | ❌ Illegal | `-` is not in alphabet |
| `module` | ❌ Illegal | Reserved keyword |
| `begin_end` | ❌ Illegal | Contains reserved `begin`/`end` substring (whole word is reserved, but compiler flags substrings in strict mode) |
| `OutPut` | ✅ Legal | Case-sensitive — different from `output` |
| `q[7:0]` | ❌ Illegal | `[` and `]` not allowed in identifiers |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State the rules for naming identifiers in Verilog. Give two examples of legal and two of illegal identifiers.*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

The rules for naming identifiers in Verilog HDL (IEEE 1364) are:
1. Identifiers may contain only letters (A–Z, a–z), digits (0–9), underscore (\_), and dollar sign ($).
2. The **first character must be a letter or an underscore** — it cannot be a digit or $.
3. Verilog is **case-sensitive**: `Output` and `output` are distinct.
4. They **cannot be reserved keywords** such as `module`, `wire`, `reg`, `always`, etc.
5. Whitespace and special characters (other than `_` and `$`) are forbidden.
6. Maximum length is implementation-dependent (typically up to 1024 characters).

**Legal examples:** `clk_100MHz`, `data_in[7:0]`, `_reset_n`, `chip$select`.
**Illegal examples:** `8bit_bus` (starts with digit), `data-bus` (hyphen not allowed), `module` (reserved keyword), `clk frequency` (contains space).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the four-value logic system of Verilog with the meaning of each value. What does `x` indicate in simulation?*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

Verilog uses a **four-value logic system** where every bit can take one of four values:

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **`0`** | Logic zero — represents LOW voltage / false / GND |
| **`1`** | Logic one — represents HIGH voltage / true / VDD |
| **`x`** | **Unknown / indeterminate logic level** |
| **`z`** | **High-impedance** — disconnected wire, tri-state buffer output |

**Meaning of `x`:** In simulation, `x` indicates an **unknown or uninitialized** condition. It arises when:
- A `reg` is declared but never assigned a value.
- Two drivers on a `wire` force **opposite values simultaneously** (contention).
- An unconnected input pin is read during simulation.
- Reading a value outside the declared bit-width.

The `x` value is essential for catching design bugs early — it flags race conditions, contention, and unconnected signals.

---

### 📕 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### ✍️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the classification of **data objects** in Verilog with suitable examples. Distinguish clearly between `wire` and `reg` data objects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various categories of **operators** in Verilog. Explain with examples the difference between **logical, bitwise, and reduction** operators. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (a) — Data Objects Classification

**Data objects** in Verilog are symbolic containers that hold values during simulation. They are classified into **three major categories**:

**1. Nets** — Represent physical connections; do not store values.

```verilog
wire  data_bus;          // Single-driver connection
wand  shared_line;       // Wired-AND bus
wor   open_emitter;      // Wired-OR bus
tri   bidirectional;     // 3-state net
supply1 VDD;             // Logic 1 power rail
```

**2. Registers** — Hold values until updated by procedural assignment.

```verilog
reg        q;            // 1-bit storage
reg [7:0]  counter;      // 8-bit register
integer    i;            // 32-bit signed
real       voltage;      // IEEE floating point
time       sim_time;     // 64-bit simulation time
```

**3. Parameters** — Compile-time constants for reusability.

```verilog
parameter WIDTH = 8;
localparam MAX = (1 << WIDTH) - 1;
```

**Distinction between `wire` and `reg`:**

| Feature | `wire` | `reg` |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | ❌ Does not store | ✅ Stores value |
| Assigned via | `assign`, gate output, port | Procedural block (`always`, `initial`) |
| Synthesis | Maps to a physical wire | Maps to flip-flop (in `always @posedge`) |
| Default value | `z` | `x` |
| Multi-driver | ✅ Allowed | ❌ Not allowed (last assignment wins) |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (b) — Operator Categories

Verilog operators fall into **9 categories**. The three categories that students commonly confuse are:

**Logical Operators** — Operate on operands as a **whole boolean**, returning 1 bit:

```verilog
module logical_demo;
  reg [3:0] a, b;
  initial begin
    a = 4'b1010; b = 4'b1100;
    $display("a && b = %b", a && b);   // Output: 1  (both non-zero)
    $display("a || b = %b", a || b);   // Output: 1
    $display("!a     = %b", !a);       // Output: 0
  end
endmodule
```

**Bitwise Operators** — Operate **bit-by-bit** on corresponding positions:

```verilog
4'b1010 & 4'b1100  = 4'b1000
4'b1010 | 4'b1100  = 4'b1110
4'b1010 ^ 4'b1100  = 4'b0110
~4'b1010           = 4'b0101
```

**Reduction Operators** — Apply the operator **across all bits** of a single operand, producing **one bit**:

```verilog
&4'b1010   = 1'b0   // 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 = 0
|4'b1010   = 1'b1   // 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 = 1
^4'b1010   = 1'b0   // 1 ^ 0 ^ 1 ^ 0 = 0
~^4'b1010  = 1'b1   // XNOR of all bits
```

**Summary Table:**

| Operator Type | Operand Count | Returns | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logical (`&&`, `\|\|`) | 2 | 1-bit boolean | `a && b` |
| Bitwise (`&`, `\|`, `^`) | 2 | Same width as operands | `a & b` (bit-by-bit) |
| Reduction (`&`, `\|`, `^`) | 1 (unary) | 1 bit | `&a` (AND of all bits) |

---

### ✍️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **four-value logic system** of Verilog. Construct the truth table for **multi-driver resolution** on a `wire` and explain the significance of `x` in catching design bugs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **operator precedence and associativity** in Verilog with an example. Evaluate the expression `a + b * c > d && e == f` for `a=2, b=4, c=1, d=5, e=0, f=1`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (a) — Four-Value Logic System

*(See Q2 of Part A for the four-value definitions.)*

**Multi-Driver Resolution Truth Table on `wire`:**

| Driver A | Driver B | Resolved Value | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Strong |
| 0 | 1 | **x** | Contention |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Strong |
| 0 | z | 0 | z is weakest |
| 1 | z | 1 | z is weakest |
| x | z | x | x propagates |
| z | z | z | All disconnected |

**Significance of `x` in catching design bugs:**
1. **Uninitialized registers** — declared `reg` variables default to `x`, alerting designers to missing initializations.
2. **Bus contention** — when two outputs drive opposite values, the simulator flags `x` immediately, preventing silent hardware damage in real circuits.
3. **Race conditions** — simultaneous blocking assignments in different `always` blocks produce `x` in simulation.
4. **Unconnected ports** — floating inputs default to `x`, warning the designer about missing connections.
5. **Out-of-range bit selection** — reading past the MSB/LSB yields `x`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `==` with `===`** — losing 1 mark per question. Remember: `==` treats `x` as unknown; `===` does exact 4-value comparison.
> 2. **Confusing `wire` and `reg`** — `reg` does NOT always mean flip-flop. In combinational `always @(*)` blocks, `reg` is just a variable.
> 3. **Forgetting `z` in the 4-value system** — many students list only `0, 1, x` and skip `z`. That costs full 3 marks.
> 4. **Using `wire` with `=` inside `always` blocks** — this is a **compile-time syntax error**.
> 5. **Writing `module` as an identifier** — always check the reserved-keyword list before declaring names.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (b) — Precedence and Associativity

**Precedence:** When multiple operators appear in an expression, higher-precedence operators evaluate first. Verilog precedence (highest to lowest):
`unary > power > * / % > + - > shift > relational > equality > bitwise AND > XOR > OR > && > || > ?:`

**Associativity:** Determines the order of evaluation when operators of **equal precedence** appear (Verilog uses **left-to-right** for almost all operators, except `?:` which is **right-to-left**).

**Evaluation of `a + b * c > d && e == f`** with `a=2, b=4, c=1, d=5, e=0, f=1`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Multiplication (precedence 3)} \\
&b \cdot c = 4 \times 1 = 4 \\
&\text{Expression: } a + 4 > d\ \&\&\ e == f \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Addition (precedence 4)} \\
&a + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 \\
&\text{Expression: } 6 > d\ \&\&\ e == f \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Relational > (precedence 6)} \\
&6 > 5 = 1\ (\text{true}) \\
&\text{Expression: } 1\ \&\&\ e == f \\
&\textbf{Step 4: Equality == (precedence 7)} \\
&e == f = (0 == 1) = 0 \\
&\text{Expression: } 1\ \&\&\ 0 \\
&\textbf{Step 5: Logical AND (precedence 11)} \\
&1\ \&\&\ 0 = \mathbf{0} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** `0`

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Identifier Rules:** Letter/underscore start, `[A-Za-z0-9_$]`, case-sensitive, no reserved keywords.
- ✅ **Three Data Object Classes:** Nets (continuous), Registers (procedural), Parameters (compile-time).
- ✅ **`wire` ≠ physical wire; `reg` ≠ flip-flop** — they are Verilog storage classes, not hardware.
- ✅ **Four-Value Logic:** `0, 1, x, z` — `x` = unknown, `z` = high-impedance.
- ✅ **Contention `0`+`1` → `x`** — never resolves to a definite value.
- ✅ **Operator Precedence Order:** Unary → Power → Multiplicative → Additive → Shift → Relational → Equality → Bitwise → Logical → Conditional.
- ✅ **`==` vs `===`:** Logical equality ignores `x`/`z`; case equality compares all four values exactly.
- ✅ **Logical vs Bitwise vs Reduction:** Whole operand vs bit-by-bit vs all-bits-collapsed-to-1.
- ✅ **Bit-Width Rule:** Result width = $\max(L_a, L_b)$; overflow bits are truncated.
- ✅ **`wire` defaults to `z`; `reg` defaults to `x`** — common viva question.
- ✅ **Best Practice:** Use `_n` suffix for active-low signals; use uppercase for parameters.
- ✅ **Reserved keywords** (memorize at least 30): `module, always, assign, wire, reg, input, output, begin, end, if, else, case, while, for, function, task, parameter, integer, real, time, supply0, supply1, and, or, not, nand, nor, xor, xnor, buf, tri, wand, wor`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates – Laws of Boolean Algebra, Postulates, Simplification of Boolean Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT402 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates – Laws of Boolean Algebra, Postulates, Simplification of Boolean Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Boolean Algebra?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Boolean Algebra is a mathematical system consisting of a set of elements **B = {0, 1}** together with two binary operations — **OR (+)** and **AND (·)** — and one unary operation **NOT (′)** — that satisfies a specific set of postulates first formalized by **Edward V. Huntington (1904)**. It is the algebraic foundation of all digital logic design.

Unlike ordinary algebra where variables can take any real value, Boolean variables are restricted to only **two logical states: 0 (FALSE / LOW / 0V)** and **1 (TRUE / HIGH / +V)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Switch" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Switch Box**
> Imagine a control panel with switches labelled **A**, **B**, and **C**, and a single light bulb **L**.
> - The bulb can only be in one of **two states**: **OFF (0)** or **ON (1)**.
> - An **AND** operation is like **two switches in series** — both must be ON for the bulb to glow.
